title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita

Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the share of children under five who are stunted and GDP per capita for various countries in 2024, with GDP per capita expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices on a logarithmic horizontal axis. The vertical axis represents the percentage of stunted children, ranging from 0% to over 50%. A general negative trend is visible, where countries with lower GDP per capita tend to have higher stunting rates. Burundi is positioned at the top left with a stunting rate above 50% and a GDP per capita below $2,000. Several African nations, including Niger, DR Congo, and the Central African Republic, cluster in the upper-left quadrant with stunting rates between 40% and 50% and GDP per capita figures generally under $3,000. In the middle of the distribution, countries like India, Pakistan, and Guatemala show stunting rates between 30% and 45% with GDP per capita ranging roughly from $8,000 to $12,000. Conversely, countries with higher GDP per capita, such as those in Europe and North America, are concentrated in the bottom right corner, exhibiting stunting rates below 10% and GDP per capita often exceeding $20,000. For instance, the United States, Canada, and most European nations appear in the lowest band of stunting prevalence. Some outliers exist, such as Timor-Leste and Angola, which have stunting rates near or above 45% despite GDP per capita levels around $5,000 to $10,000. The data points are color-coded by region, showing that African countries (purple) dominate the high-stunting, low-GDP area, while European (blue) and North American (orange) countries occupy the low-stunting, high-GDP area.
